whatThisIs: Reference the four-class Mallampati airway score used to predict difficult laryngoscopy. Class I shows the soft palate, fauces, uvula, and both tonsillar pillars; Class II shows the soft palate, fauces, and uvula; Class III shows the soft palate and base of the uvula; Class IV shows only the hard palate. The patient sits upright, opens the mouth maximally, and protrudes the tongue without phonating. Sophie Well lists each class with its standard short description and the original Mallampati / Samsoon-Young source attribution.
whenToUse: Use this when conducting a pre-anesthesia airway assessment, evaluating a patient before procedural sedation in the ED, or teaching the components of a difficult-airway exam alongside thyromental distance, mouth opening, neck extension, and the LEMON / MOANS mnemonics. Mallampati is one input - not the answer - to the difficult-airway question. Class III and IV raise prior probability of difficult intubation but a low class does not rule it out, and the test has lower interrater reliability than its ubiquity suggests.
